completed at a meeting of the as |
ISOLATION last night. The picnic |
will be held at Liberty lake Thurs |
day, June %. All groceries and |
meat shops will be closed on that |
day.


Simon Green, fiscal agent for the
United Wireless Telegraph COM
pany, says that his company is glad
of the federal investigation being
conducted into its affairs. Between
$500 and 5000 people in the Inland |
Empire are interested in the stock
OF the Wireless.


D. j. Hayward, juror in Judge
Huneke's court, was late yesterday,
but was excused when he said that
he became entangled in a runaway.
When he arrived at court he was
withuot a necktie.


Miss Grace Henry has been APP
pointed secretary to Captain w. II.
Apetz of the Spokane Volunteers of
America. -


{. e. Chapman, the worse for too
much beer or other intOxicant, tried
to give a large part of the police
force cigars last night. All refused,
and he was locked up to sober up.


George A. Lee, assistant attorney
general of Washington, has return
ed from an eastern trip. He said
that fasteners are much interest
ed in Spokane.


Judge Edward Whitson has hand
ed down a decision confirming the
rights of certain Indians, members
of the Yakima tribe, to their home
steads in the Yakima reservation.
Secretary Rem n. Rice of the Ma
tional Apple show left last night
on a business trip to eastern Mon
tana. Saturday he returned from a
| trip to the Bitter Root valley and
reported that interest is strong in
the coming apple show.


L. r. Horton, anti-saIoon league
superintendent, returned from
Walla Walla yesterday. The AL
Saloon league field day will be held
there next Sunday.


A non-suit was announced yester-
day in the case of G. H. Grange
and ]. A. Hodgins. Their wagon
was struck by an Inland Empire
car, but Judge Hinkle ruled that
when a driver tries to cross in
front of a train they were guilty
OF contributory negligence.


Hiram Smith left his wife a short
time ago, and she told the prose
cutting attorneys office about it,.
! Yesterday Smith told MS story. He
said that he had spent his wages
on the Lowing bowl" and did not
dare to go home. So he went to
Butte, got better job there, and
when he mad saved little money,
returned to his wife with it.


Methodist ministers Of the city
picnicked today at Liberty lake.


Work on the new grade schools
and the addition to the North Cen
trail high school is progressing rap
idly and will probably be complet-
ed in time for the reopening of
school in September. Right at the
